title: Causal Relationships Between Screen Use, Reading, and Brain Development in Early Adolescents.
abstract: The rise of new media has greatly changed the lifestyles, leading to increased time on these platforms and less time spent reading. This shift has particularly profound impacts on early adolescents, who are in a critical stage of brain development. Previous studies have found associations between screen use and mental health, but it remains unclear whether screen use is the direct cause of the outcomes. Here, the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) dataset is utlized to examine the causal relationships between screen use and brain development. The results revealed adverse causal effects of screen use on language ability and specific behaviors in early adolescents, while reading has positive causal effects on their language ability and brain volume in the frontal and temporal regions. Interestingly, increased screen use is identified as a result, rather than a cause, of certain behaviors such as rule-breaking and aggressive behaviors. Furthermore, the analysis uncovered an indirect influence of screen use, mediated by changes in reading habits, on brain development. These findings provide new evidence for the causal influences of screen use on brain development and highlight the importance of monitoring media use and related habit change in children.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, approximately 12,000 participants aged 9-13 years at baseline, 2-year follow-up (n=455 at 2-year follow-up from initial n=707)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use types measured separately: TV watching (TVMs), Video (watching videos), Game (video games), Text (cell phone texting), SNS (social networking sites), VC (video chatting); also reading time. Platform-specific measurements extractable from analyses.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects (LME) models and Mendelian randomization analyses reporting causal effects. Regression coefficients for screen use on cognitive/behavioral outcomes: TVM on picture vocabulary β=-2.39 (corrected p<0.02), TVM on withdrawn/depressed β=0.57 (corrected p<0.05), VC on behavioral problems β=-0.30 (corrected p<0.0006)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple effect coefficients with p-values reported: β=-2.39 (corrected p<0.02), β=0.57 (corrected p<0.05), β=-0.30 (corrected p<0.0006), and numerous Mendelian randomization coefficients in Table S2 with credible intervals

FT_IC5.evidence: Original longitudinal empirical analysis using ABCD data with causal inference methods (LME models, genetic instrumental variables, Mendelian randomization, two-sample MR analysis)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Advanced Science, 2024), not a preprint or duplicate

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports multiple screen use→outcome associations (screen time predicting cognitive and behavioral outcomes), and includes both cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses. FT-EC7 would only apply if screen use were exclusively the dependent variable with only demographic predictors in all analyses. Instead, this paper primarily uses screen use/reading as exposures predicting brain development, cognition, and behavior outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract mentions ABCD dataset but PDF not yet reviewed.

title: Prospective association of screen time with binge-eating disorder among adolescents in the United States: The mediating role of depression.
journal: The International journal of eating disorders
abstract: OBJECTIVE: Screen time has been reported to be associated with binge-eating disorder (BED) among adolescents in the US; however, potential mediators remain unclear. This study aimed to evaluate depression symptoms as a mediator of the prospective association between screen time and BED. METHOD: We utilized data from 9465 children (aged 9-11âyears at baseline) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study (2016-2021). A generalized structural equation model was used to examine the prospective association between average daily screen time at baseline and BED at year 2, adjusting for baseline BED diagnosis, and other potential covariates (e.g., age, sex, and income). Mediation was examined using bias-corrected (BC) 95% confidence intervals for the indirect effect of baseline screen time on year 2 BED through depression symptoms (change from baseline to year 1). RESULTS: One hundred and one participants (42.7% male, 49.4% racial/ethnic minority) met the criteria for BED in year 2. Participants were 9.9âyears of age on average at baseline, 51.3% identified as male, and 43.1% identified as a racial/ethnic minority. Adjusting for covariates, screen time was prospectively associated with BED (ORâ=â1.09, 95% CI [1.03, 1.14], pâ=â.005). Depression symptoms (Bâ=â.19, BC 95% CI [0.10, 0.28]) partially mediated (9.2%) the prospective association between screen time and BED. DISCUSSION: Among US adolescents, higher baseline screen time was prospectively associated with BED diagnosis at year 2, and this relationship was partially mediated by increased depression symptoms. Preventive approaches targeting high screen use may have utility for reducing BED risk among adolescents. PUBLIC SIGNIFICANCE: Among U.S. adolescents, higher screen time was prospectively associated with the incidence of BED. This association was partially mediated by the change in depressive symptoms. Preventive approaches targeting high screen use may have utility for reducing BED risk among adolescents.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (2016–2021); explicitly stated: 'We utilized data from 9465 children (aged 9–11 years at baseline) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/screen time variable: 'average daily screen time at baseline'; NOT broken down by device or platform type in abstract, but qualifies as 'screen time' measure. However, abstract does not clarify smartphone-specificity vs general screen time. Reading abstract carefully: 'screen time' without smartphone specification. Per FT-IC2 rules, 'generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown' = NOT_MET. UNKNOWN pending PDF confirmation of whether smartphone-specific coefficient is separately reported.

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized structural equation model with prospective association: 'screen time was prospectively associated with BED (OR = 1.09, 95% CI [1.03, 1.14], p = .005)' linking screen time (SM/phone variable) to binge-eating disorder (non-SM outcome)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric statistics reported: OR = 1.09 with 95% CI [1.03, 1.14] for screen time↔BED; mediation coefficient B = .19 with BC 95% CI [0.10, 0.28]; p-values reported

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis examining screen time and BED with mediation model; longitudinal design (baseline to year 2)

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (International Journal of Eating Disorders, 2024); not a preprint or duplicate

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Screen time is not the dependent variable; it is the independent variable predicting BED. Paper includes a psychological/clinical outcome (BED = eating disorder), not only demographics. Longitudinal prospective design with individual clinical predictor.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data; N=9465 children (aged 9-11 years at baseline) followed 2016-2021.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures 'average daily screen time' as composite variable with no phone/SM breakdown. Abstract states 'screen time' but does not specify smartphone, social media, or other qualifying modalities. Composite screen time without SM-specific coefficient is exclusionary per rule.

FT_IC3.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract indicates prospective association between screen time and BED (OR=1.09, 95% CI [1.03, 1.14]) and mediation by depression, but cannot verify full analysis without PDF.

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract reports OR and CI values, but cannot verify extractability or completeness without document.

FT_IC5.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract suggests empirical analysis (generalized SEM mediation analysis), but full methodology cannot be confirmed.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (International Journal of Eating Disorders, 2024). Not identified as duplicate/preprint.

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Excluded at FT-IC2 (composite screen time without phone/SM breakdown). FT-EC7 irrelevant.

title: Associations of Screen Time and Physical Activity With Body Mass Index in Early Adolescence: A Prospective Cohort Study.
abstract: OBJECTIVE: This study aimed toÂ prospectively identify the independent, mutually adjusted, and interactive associations of screen time and physical activity with BMI and overweight/obesity risk in adolescents. METHODS: This study analyzed prospective data from 5356âUS adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 (2018-2020, ages 11-12) and Year 4 (2020-2022, ages 13-14). RESULTS: Mean daily screen time was 6.1 (Â±5.2) hours and mean step count was 9265 (Â±3227) steps in Year 2; 32.7% were classified with overweight/obesity in Year 4. In Poisson regression models examining the exposures mutually adjusted for each other, high screen time (>â8âh/day) was associated with higher risk of overweight/obesity (risk ratio [RR], 1.09; 95% CI, 1.02-1.16; pâ=â0.013) compared with low screen time (0-4âh/day). In multivariable linear and Poisson regression models, respectively, low step count (1000-6000 steps/day) was associated with higher BMI percentile (coefficient [B], 3.27; 95% CI, 1.54-4.99; pâ<â0.001) and higher risk of overweight/obesity (RR, 1.23; 95% CI, 1.07-1.40; pâ=â0.003) compared with high step count (>â12,000 steps/day). No significant interactions were observed between screen time and step count. CONCLUSIONS: These findings suggest that obesity prevention efforts should consider both greater physical activity and less recreational screen use as behavioral interventions.

FT_IC1.evidence: Prospective ABCD Study Years 2 and 4: 'This study analyzed prospective data from 5356 US adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 (2018–2020, ages 11–12) and Year 4 (2020–2022, ages 13–14)'

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as generic 'total recreational screen time' composite (watching TV/videos, playing video games, texting, social media, video chat) without phone/SM-specific breakdown reported separately. Fails FT-IC2 as no phone/SM-specific coefficient extractable; only aggregate composite

FT_IC3.evidence: Poisson regression and linear regression: 'high screen time (>8h/day) was associated with higher risk of overweight/obesity (risk ratio [RR], 1.09; 95% CI, 1.02–1.16; p=0.013)' and 'low step count (1000–6000 steps/day) was associated with higher BMI percentile (coefficient [B], 3.27; 95% CI, 1.54–4.99; p<0.001)' linking exposures to BMI outcome

FT_IC4.evidence: RR 1.09 (95% CI 1.02–1.16, p=0.013) for high screen time; B 3.27 (95% CI 1.54–4.99, p<0.001) for low step count on BMI percentile; multiple p-values and CIs reported in Table 2

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis: 'prospective data from 5356 US adolescents...Year 2 (2018–2020)...Year 4 (2020–2022)' with Poisson and linear regression models

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (Obesity, 2026); no evidence of preprint or duplicate

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports screen time → BMI outcome association (not SM as DV); includes individual behavioral/physical outcome measure (step count); no demographic-only predictors

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (N=5356 US adolescents, Year 2: 2018-2020, Year 4: 2020-2022)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures self-reported recreational screen time via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey, categorized into 0-4h (low), 4-8h (medium), >8h (high) daily. Surveys ask about watching television and videos, playing video games, texting, social media, video chat

FT_IC3.evidence: Poisson regression models examining screen time associations with BMI percentile and overweight/obesity risk. Linear and multivariable regression models tested dose-response relationships. Example: high screen time (>8h/day) associated with overweight/obesity risk (RR 1.09; 95% CI 1.02-1.16; p=0.013)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric statistics reported: RR 1.09 (95% CI 1.02-1.16, p=0.013) for high vs low screen time; coefficient B values for BMI percentile; continuous variables also examined with reported p-values

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis with 2-year follow-up (Year 2 to Year 4)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Obesity (2026), original research article (not preprint or duplicate)

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable (predictor) and BMI/overweight-obesity are dependent variables (outcomes). Study is prospective but FT_EC7 only applies if screen time is DV with demographic-only predictors in longitudinal design. Here screen time is IV predicting health outcome
